An engineer tries to specify how much “memory” a system may use. The obvious move is to count parameters, but the premises block that simplification. Memory cannot be budgeted cleanly in isolation because compute, latency, energy, and auditability are equally constraining. So any acceptable definition of memory must be multidimensional: a change that saves parameters but increases inference-time state, retraining cost, logging burden, or energy use may violate the budget anyway.

Now the engineer studies updates. An update cannot be labeled simply “good transfer” or “bad interference.” At one scale it improves behavior in one layer, while at another it degrades another layer. So the effect of adaptation is not globally classifiable. Whether the system is improving depends on where one measures and at what granularity. This means auditing adaptation is difficult twice over: first because the budget is multidimensional, second because the same update has mixed status across the model.

Next comes test-time adaptation. Initially it seems allowable because it is temporary: the model adjusts at inference and then reverts. But the premise says the boundary with continual learning disappears once temporary adjustments leave permanent residues. Therefore, if any test-time state persists in a way that changes future behavior, the system is no longer merely adapting at test time; it is learning continuously. That persistence must now be counted in the memory budget, not just as stored weights but as compute paths, latent state, audit trail, and energy costs of maintaining or examining those residues.

Put together, the world implied is one where memory policy cannot be a static cap on model size. It must govern parameter changes, transient state, persistence mechanisms, and the cost of reconstructing what happened. Because updates can help one layer and harm another, no single scalar metric can certify that adaptation stayed beneficial. And because temporary changes may become residues, even a system advertised as non-learning at deployment may in practice become a continual learner.

The remaining premises introduce additional events or relations: “Slockster sebastodes parrier chuet the oscillometer” and “Squeel lipa trimestrial mailcatcher conia the dusk.” Since these terms are otherwise undefined, the only strict implication is that in this world those propositions are also true. They do not alter the previous conclusions unless further meanings are supplied. So the collision is asymmetrical: the technical premises force a complicated, unstable boundary around memory and learning, while the two opaque premises stand as true but uninterpreted facts within the same world.
